canonical_definition: FairEval is described in multiple distinct ways across the surveyed literature. The most prevalent framing, found in several papers, is as a benchmark dataset for evaluating open-ended question answering. In this capacity, it is reported to consist of 80 questions from diverse categories like "common-sense, counterfactual, coding" and is explicitly used to measure the "Open-ended question answering" behavior. A different paper presents FairEval as a "meta-evaluation benchmark" for assessing the performance and biases of LLM evaluators, noting its average human annotation accuracy is 71.7%. Yet another study frames it differently, as a "fairness-oriented evaluation procedure" adopted specifically to "eliminate positional bias." These varied descriptions show that FairEval is operationalized as a task-specific dataset, a tool for meta-evaluation, and a procedural method for assessment.
papers: ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate
Evaluating Large Language Models at Evaluating Instruction Following
On the Resilience of LLM-Based Multi-Agent Collaboration with Faulty Agents
SNS-Bench: Defining, Building, and Assessing Capabilities of Large Language Models in Social Networking Services

definitions.text: A benchmark dataset for evaluating open-ended question answering, consisting of 80 questions from a wide array of categories and corresponding human annotations.
definitions.paper: ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate

relationships.paper: ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate
relationships.evidence: We evaluate ChatEval on two benchmarks, FairEval and Topical-Chat which represent the categories of open-ended question answer and dialogue response generation, respectively. (Section 3)

iclr24: ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate
